title: A resolution supporting the goals and ideals of National Public Health Week.
plain_summary: This resolution supports National Public Health Week, which celebrates the work done to keep Americans healthy. The resolution talks about big health problems that Americans face and the good things that have happened in public health over the last thirty years. It worries about cuts to health agencies that help protect people from getting sick. The resolution asks Congress to keep spending money on public health programs that prevent disease and help make sure all Americans can be healthy.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Resolution Supporting the Goals and Ideals of National Public Health Week
sections.text:
This resolution supports National Public Health Week, which happens during the week of April seventh, two thousand twenty five. The theme for two thousand twenty five is called "It Starts Here" and focuses on making a difference in our homes and communities.

The resolution recognizes that public health work helps improve the health of all Americans and works toward health equity, which means everyone has a fair chance to be healthy. However, there are serious health challenges facing our country.

Life expectancy in the United States dropped by two point seven years between two thousand nineteen and two thousand twenty one. This was the biggest two year drop since nineteen twenty one to nineteen twenty three. Many deaths come from chronic diseases, which are long lasting health conditions that are often preventable.

There are big differences in health between the healthiest and least healthy states. People in some states have much higher rates of obesity, chronic disease, and infectious disease than others. Racial and ethnic minority groups continue to have worse health outcomes and die at higher rates than the overall population.

Violence is a major cause of early death. More than eight people die violent deaths every hour in the United States. Violence costs the economy billions of dollars and causes trauma, injury, disability, depression, anxiety, and post traumatic stress disorder in communities.

In two thousand twenty two, forty nine thousand four hundred seventy six people died by suicide. More than fifty four percent of these suicides involved firearms.

While infant deaths have dropped to a historic low of five point six deaths per one thousand births in two thousand twenty two, there are still major differences by race and income. Black babies die at twice the rate of white babies.

Women in the United States die from pregnancy related problems at higher rates than in many other developed countries. The maternal death rate was twenty two point three deaths per one hundred thousand births in two thousand twenty two. About eighty percent of these deaths could have been prevented. American Indian, Alaska Native, and Black mothers die at much higher rates than white mothers.

Drug overdose deaths reached one hundred seven thousand nine hundred forty one in two thousand twenty two. This was a seventeen percent increase from two thousand twenty and more than double the rate from two thousand fifteen.

Cigarette smoking causes more than four hundred eighty thousand deaths every year and is the leading cause of preventable disease and death. However, adult smoking rates have dropped from twenty point nine percent in two thousand five to eleven point six percent in two thousand twenty two. E-cigarettes have been the most common tobacco product used by young people since two thousand fourteen, with seven point eight percent of high school students reporting e-cigarette use in two thousand twenty four.

Air pollution from tiny particles caused about thirty two thousand deaths in two thousand twenty, with thirty seven percent directly related to burning fossil fuels. Heat related deaths increased by about seventy four percent when comparing two thousand seventeen to two thousand twenty one with two thousand to two thousand four.

Voting helps create conditions where people can be healthy. Good health makes people more likely to vote, but only sixty four percent of eligible adults reported voting in the November two thousand twenty four election.

This year marks the thirtieth anniversary of National Public Health Week. Over the past thirty years, there have been major improvements in public health including greatly reducing HIV and AIDS deaths, decreasing smoking rates, reducing childhood deaths, increasing health insurance coverage, improving mental health awareness and treatment, reducing lead poisoning in children, decreasing antibiotic misuse, addressing climate change health impacts, and improving pandemic preparedness.

The resolution expresses concern about proposed cuts to Medicaid and changes to the Department of Health and Human Services that would reduce staff and combine several agencies. It also notes that the National Institutes of Health and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention have faced staff and funding cuts that limit their ability to respond to health threats like measles outbreaks and future disease outbreaks.

The resolution emphasizes that public health organizations use National Public Health Week to educate policymakers about health issues. Small investments in disease prevention can save significant healthcare costs. Vaccination is one of the greatest public health achievements and has greatly reduced disease, hospitalizations, deaths, and healthcare costs.

Public health professionals help communities prevent, prepare for, and recover from health threats including disease outbreaks, natural disasters, and public health emergencies. They work with partners outside healthcare like city planners, transportation officials, educators, and businesses because these other sectors also affect health.

People across the United States are changing how they care for their health by avoiding tobacco, eating healthier, being more physically active, and preventing injuries at home and work. Supporting public health and prevention can transform our health system from one focused on treating illness to one focused on preventing disease and promoting wellness.

Therefore, the Senate supports the goals of National Public Health Week, recognizes the efforts of public health professionals and others in preventing disease and injury, acknowledges the role of public health in responding to outbreaks and addressing health disparities, encourages increased efforts to improve health and make the United States the healthiest nation in the world within one generation, and encourages Americans to learn about how the public health system improves health across the country.
